images.caption: Figure 1:Overview of the proposed SHAMISA framework.(1) Self-supervised pre-training:pristine images are transformed by a compositional distortion engine to form a mini-batch𝐗\mathbf{X}and distortion metadata; the encoderfθf_{\theta}and projectorgψg_{\psi}produce representations𝐇\mathbf{H}and embeddings𝐙\mathbf{Z}used to construct dual-source relation graphs (metadata-driven and structurally intrinsic), which are aggregated by a multi-source aggregatorΦ\Phiinto𝐆\mathbf{G}and optimize a graph-weighted SSL objective.(2) Downstream NR-IQA:we freezefθf_{\theta}and train a lightweight regressor on top of𝐇\mathbf{H}to predict quality scores.

Figure 2:gMAD on Waterloo Exploration (distorted pool):panels (a,b) use SHAMISA as the defender and panels (c,d) use ARNIQA as the defender. In each panel, the top and bottom images are the attacker-selected pair from the indicated defender-quality bin, with low-bin cases shown in (a,c) and high-bin cases shown in (b,d). Supplementary Table VII reports the corresponding top-1 attacker gaps.

Figure 3:t-SNE visualization of image-level encoder representations on KADID-10K.We extract encoder representations𝐇\mathbf{H}(pre-projector) and average across crops to obtain one representation per image, yielding 10,125 image-level points.
Points are colored by the coarse distortion family using a shared fixed palette.
Compared to ARNIQA, SHAMISA exhibits clearer coarse-family structure for several dominant degradation families, while both models show overlap among visually related families such as brightness and color manipulations.
This visualization is a qualitative diagnostic and does not affect the quantitative evaluation protocol.

Figure 5:SRCC vs. training steps during SSL pre-training(mean with±\pmstd error bars over 10 splits) across representative NR-IQA benchmarks.
Curves typically rise early and then saturate, while the best checkpoint can vary by dataset since the SSL objective is not identical to downstream SRCC.

Figure 6:One-at-a-time sweeps around the SHAMISA reference A0,reporting SRCC across six NR-IQA benchmarks in a single merged view. Columns correspond to(dh,dz)(d_{h},d_{z}),(B,ξ)(B,\xi),(α,β)(\alpha,\beta),(γ,η)(\gamma,\eta), and(kn,K)(k_{n},K), with a shared legend across all panels.
Figure 7:t-SNE colored by distortion severity on KADID-10K.Colors indicate severity levels 1 to 5 for ARNIQA (left) and SHAMISA (right).
Figure 8:Subtype-colored t-SNE views for SHAMISA, including six KADID-10K distortion families.

Figure 10:Effective dimensionality proxy (rank) forH{\bm{H}}over SSL pre-training.The encoder representation uses a substantial number of active dimensions throughout training and then stabilizes after the early phase.
